clinical_history: Elderly man with a previously functional electronic pacemaker. What is the rhythm at present?
diagnosis: The ECG shows a narrow (QRS) complex, very regular bradycardia at about 40/min, indicating pacemaker malfunction. The atria are fibrillating, not in flutter; the latter produces an unchanging atrial pattern from one section to next. The diffuse T wave inversions (but not in leads I and aVL) are consistent with "T wave memory" from intermittent RV pacing. (See, e.g., Shvilkin A et al. Circulation. 2005;111:969-74.) His paced ECGs showed positive QRS morphologies in aVL and I and negative QRS complexes in V1-V6, consistent with the polarity of the post-pacing (memory) T waves, which tends to be concordant with vector of the QRS during prior pacing.  The limb leads show non-specifically low voltage.
